one_line_takeaway: A broad PRISMA survey (217 papers) that unifies FL security and privacy under one attack/defense taxonomy and adds an original comparison of 13 FL frameworks, arguing prior surveys treated these dimensions in isolation.
key_finding: The field is fragmented: of 16 prior surveys only 1 covered both privacy and security, fewer than half offered any structured attack/defense taxonomy, and 12 of 16 omitted practical concerns such as frameworks or real-world applications. Substantively, model poisoning generally out-damages data poisoning and can defeat Byzantine-robust aggregators; robust operators (Krum, Bulyan, Trimmed Mean, GeoMed) are individually beatable by variance-exploiting attacks like 'A Little Is Enough' and IPM; and privacy defenses trade utility/compute for protection (HE and MPC are strong but costly, DP hurts underrepresented classes, hybrid DP-MPC offers the best efficiency-privacy balance). Non-IID data is identified as the cross-cutting factor that both worsens attacks and degrades defenses.
core_contribution: A systematic literature survey (PRISMA-based) of security and privacy in Federated Learning that unifies attacks, defenses, frameworks, application domains, and future directions under a single taxonomy. The authors' OWN contributions (as opposed to the primary studies they catalog) are: (1) a two-branch taxonomy separating 'security-enhancing' threats/defenses (Byzantine/poisoning, GAN-based, Sybil, backdoor, free-riding, jamming, evasion, straggling, dropout; defended by robust aggregation, adversarial training, pruning, personalization) from 'privacy-preserving' ones (gradient inversion, membership/property inference, GAN-based inference, eavesdropping; defended by DP, HE, MPC, secret sharing, ZKP, gradient clipping/noise); (2) a five-dimension classification scheme for security attacks (target specificity, phase affected, intent, nature of adversary, execution style) rendered in Table 4; (3) a hands-on feature comparison of 13 FL frameworks (Table 9); and (4) a comparison table (Table 1) positioning this survey against 16 prior surveys, claiming to be the only one covering security AND privacy taxonomies, frameworks, applications, and future directions together.
threat_model: The survey adopts the standard FL adversary model rather than proposing a new one. Adversaries may be insiders (malicious/curious clients or a compromised aggregation server) or outsiders (network attackers, eavesdroppers). Capabilities range from continuous multi-round participation (poisoning, backdoor, Sybil) to one-shot disruption (jamming, evasion at inference). Security objectives at stake are integrity, availability, and confidentiality of the global model; privacy objectives concern preventing reconstruction of clients' raw data from shared gradients/updates. A recurring theme is that non-IID (statistically heterogeneous) client data amplifies both poisoning impact and inference leakage while weakening classical defenses (DP, median/trimmed-mean aggregation, anomaly detection).
limitations: As a survey it contributes no new empirical validation, so all reported effectiveness numbers inherit the assumptions and setups of their source papers and are not compared on a common benchmark (the authors themselves flag the lack of unified benchmarks and inconsistent attack coverage as an open problem). The framework comparison (Table 9) is a qualitative feature checklist rather than a measured performance/security evaluation, and reflects a snapshot that will age as frameworks evolve. The taxonomy's five attack dimensions overlap in practice (the authors acknowledge real attacks span multiple categories and classify by 'principal form'). 6), Future Directions (Sec. 7), and Conclusion (Sec. 8) were only partially represented.

datasets_used: None generated by the survey authors (no experiments run); MNIST, and application-domain datasets appear only within the cited primary studies the survey summarizes
quantitative_results: Corpus: 59 search queries over 6 databases; 2,002 papers screened; narrowed to 217 papers with impact score > 5; 16 prior surveys compared in Table 1; 13 FL frameworks compared in Table 9 (all authors' own synthesis)
CITED (Fung et al., FoolsGold): 2 Sybil nodes caused 96.2% of MNIST digit-1s to be misclassified as 7s
CITED (PoisonGAN, Zhang et al.): global model retained >80% accuracy on both the poisoning and primary tasks under GAN-based attack
CITED (Kim et al.): personalized federated adversarial training improved robustness ~60% vs. standard approaches against internal evasion attacks
CITED (AutoGM, Li et al.): maintained high performance with up to 30% of nodes doing model poisoning or 50% doing data poisoning
CITED (evasion, Goodfellow et al.): imperceptible perturbation made GoogLeNet misclassify a panda as a gibbon with 99.3% confidence
CITED (privacy comparative analysis): DP-MPC 16-182x faster than baseline DP; MPC+DP improves communication efficiency 56-794x; HE reaches 99.95% accuracy on MNIST at high compute cost
CITED (Flower framework): scales to up to 15 million clients on a pair of high-end GPUs
baselines_compared: Robust aggregation operators surveyed: FedAvg (baseline), Trimmed Mean, Median/GeoMed/Medoid/MarMed/MeaMed, AutoGM, Krum/Multi-Krum, Bulyan, ClippedClustering, Zeno/Zeno++
Privacy defenses surveyed: Differential Privacy, Homomorphic Encryption, Secure Multiparty Computation, Secret Sharing, Zero-Knowledge Proofs, gradient clipping, additive noise, DP-MPC hybrid
FL frameworks compared (Table 9): CrypTen, FATE, FedML, FedScale, FL-and-DP, Flower, FLUTE, NVFLARE, OpenFL, PaddleFL, PySyft, TFF, XFL
Attacks catalogued as adversaries against these defenses: data/model poisoning, GAN-based, Sybil, backdoor (Chameleon, A3FL), free-riding (AFR/SFR), jamming, evasion (ADIs), straggling, dropout
